channel.title: NPR Topics: National
channel.description: NPR coverage of national news, U.S. politics, elections, business, arts, culture, health and science, and technology. Subscribe to the NPR Nation RSS feed.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: The Final Four is set with UConn stunning Duke to join Illinois, Arizona and Michigan
item.description: The Huskies beat Duke with a 3-pointer from the logo with 0.4 seconds left by Braylon Mullins, who grew up just outside of Indianapolis.
item.content:encoded:
The Huskies beat Duke with a 3-pointer from the logo with 0.4 seconds left by Braylon Mullins, who grew up just outside of Indianapolis.

(Image credit: Stephanie Scarbrough)
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press

item.title: ICE officers could remain at airports after TSA workers are paid
item.description: Even when Transportation Security Administration workers get paid, Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents could still be present at U.S. airports.
item.content:encoded:
Even when Transportation Security Administration workers get paid, Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents could still be present at U.S. airports.

(Image credit: David J. Phillip)
item.dc:creator: Jeff Brady

item.title: How young people are navigating a tough and rapidly changing job market
item.description: Finding a job is hard right now, especially for young people starting their careers. NPR's Adrian Ma spoke with college students and an economist about navigating today's tough job market.
item.content:encoded: Finding a job is hard right now, especially for young people starting their careers. NPR's Adrian Ma spoke with college students and an economist about navigating today's tough job market.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levitt

item.title: Understanding why some Iranian Americans support the war on their country of origin
item.description: Protesters from the Iranian diaspora in the U.S. gathered in Washington, D.C. as the war in the Middle East broadens. Many say they are aligned with the U.S. and Israel and explain why they want to see regime change in Tehran.
item.content:encoded: Protesters from the Iranian diaspora in the U.S. gathered in Washington, D.C. as the war in the Middle East broadens. Many say they are aligned with the U.S. and Israel and explain why they want to see regime change in Tehran.
item.dc:creator: Adrian Ma

item.title: Controversy growing over buoys in the Rio Grande
item.description: Trump administration started deploying 500 miles of Big buoys in the middle of the Rio Grande, the dividing border line with Mexico, financed by 2025's spending bill. Locals aren't happy considering that illegal border crossings are at a 50-year low.
item.content:encoded: Trump administration started deploying 500 miles of Big buoys in the middle of the Rio Grande, the dividing border line with Mexico, financed by 2025's spending bill. Locals aren't happy considering that illegal border crossings are at a 50-year low.
item.dc:creator: David Martin Davies

item.title: The view from No Kings protests across the nation, from NPR member station reporters
item.description: Protesters showed up to thousands of events across the country this weekend to air their complaints against the Trump administration.
item.content:encoded: Protesters showed up to thousands of events across the country this weekend to air their complaints against the Trump administration.

item.title: Secretary-Treasurer of the union representing TSA workers discusses the shutdown
item.description: NPR's Don Gonyea talks with Johnny Jones, of the American Federation of Government Employees union, about the training TSA agents get and the stress they've been under during government shutdowns.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Don Gonyea talks with Johnny Jones, of the American Federation of Government Employees union, about the training TSA agents get and the stress they've been under during government shutdowns.
item.dc:creator: Don Gonyea

item.title: March 31, formerly César Chávez Day, is now Farmworkers Day in California
item.description: March 31, formerly celebrated as César Chávez Day, is now Farmworkers Day in California. NPR's Don Gonyea talks to Oliver Rosales, a history professor at Bakersfield College.
item.content:encoded: March 31, formerly celebrated as César Chávez Day, is now Farmworkers Day in California. NPR's Don Gonyea talks to Oliver Rosales, a history professor at Bakersfield College.
item.dc:creator: Don Gonyea

item.title: Why a 98-year-old federal judge is asking the Supreme Court for her job back
item.description: Pauline Newman's story shines a light on the aging judiciary, where judges are getting older and lifetime tenure is raising thorny questions about retirement.
item.content:encoded:
Pauline Newman's story shines a light on the aging judiciary, where judges are getting older and lifetime tenure is raising thorny questions about retirement.

(Image credit: Paul J. Richards)
item.dc:creator: Carrie Johnson

item.title: Some critics of birthright citizenship say it's a fraud issue. What does that mean?
item.description: Advocates for ending birthright citizenship point to "birth tourism" schemes to argue that the legal principle is ripe for exploitation and threatens national security. Experts say it's not so simple.
item.content:encoded:
Advocates for ending birthright citizenship point to "birth tourism" schemes to argue that the legal principle is ripe for exploitation and threatens national security. Experts say it's not so simple.

(Image credit: Mark Schiefelbein)
item.dc:creator: Juliana Kim
